title: Wood movement and moisture
subtitle: How wood shrinks and swells with changes in humidity, and how to design around it
excerpt: A guide to how wood moves with changes in moisture content: which directions shrink and by how much, how seasonal humidity cycles cause movement in finished pieces, and how panel and frame construction accommodates movement without splitting.
sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery for moisture content, shrinkage rates, and panel construction. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for timber movement and seasoning behaviour.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried wood at a stable moisture content, dimensionally stable for joinery and box-making.
Freshly felled wood, still high in moisture content. Shrinks considerably as it dries and is not dimensionally stable for precise joinery.

content.text: Wood moves throughout its life in response to changes in the moisture content of the air. Even fully
timber in a finished piece continues to absorb moisture when the air is humid and release it when the air is dry. Understanding this movement prevents splits, sticking drawers, and joint failures.

Wood moves differently in three directions. Along the grain (lengthwise): almost no movement, less than 0.1 percent. Radially (toward the bark, across the rings): moderate movement, roughly 3 to 5 percent from green to dry. Tangentially (parallel to the growth rings): the most movement, roughly 6 to 10 percent. A board cut with its face parallel to the growth rings (flatsawn) moves more across its width than a board with its face cutting across the rings (quartersawn).

wood dries to its equilibrium moisture content. A 30 cm wide flatsawn board may shrink 20 mm across its width as it dries. This is why joinery is made from seasoned timber: the movement has already happened before cutting.

Seasoned timber still moves with seasonal humidity changes. In a centrally heated house, the air in winter is drier than in summer. A wide panel can move 3 to 5 mm across its width between summer and winter. This is normal and expected. A 200-year-old tabletop is still moving.

Frame-and-panel construction accommodates movement by floating the panel inside a groove in the surrounding frame. The panel is not glued in the groove; it is left free to move. The frame is stable because its members run in different directions and their movements cancel out. If the panel were glued in, its seasonal movement would split the frame.

For wide tabletops and shelves, attach the top to its base with slotted wooden buttons or elongated screw holes rather than through-screws. The buttons locate in a groove in the apron and slide sideways as the top moves. Through-screws in a rigid joint would split the top as the wood moves seasonally.

Quartersawn boards (rings visible as roughly vertical lines on the face) move less across their width than flatsawn boards and are more stable for tabletops and door panels. They show a straight grain figure on the face. Flatsawn boards show a wider cathedral grain figure but move more. For projects where movement matters, quartersawn is the better choice even if it is harder to source.
